title: Inspecting and maintaining a loft cold water tank
subtitle: Checking for contamination, lagging the tank and pipes, and deciding when to replace
excerpt: A cold water storage tank in the loft feeds the hot water cylinder, cold taps in older houses, and often the heating system make-up. Most tanks are polyethylene or galvanised steel, holding 100-230 litres. An annual visual inspection takes 15 minutes and catches the two main failure modes: contamination and frost damage.
sourceNotes: ACOP L8 and HSG274 (Legionella control in the UK) for cold water tank maintenance requirements. Gov.uk water regulations for plumbing standards. CIPHE (Chartered Institute of Plumbing and Heating Engineering) guidance on cold water storage tanks. Energy Saving Trust guidance on pipe lagging for frost protection.

projectSchedule.title: Annual tank inspection
projectSchedule.body: Lift the tank lid and inspect the water surface and tank walls. Check the ball valve is floating correctly, the inlet pipe screen is clear, and there is no sediment, algae, or animal matter in the tank.

content.text: A cold water storage tank in the loft holds a reserve of water for the hot water cylinder, cold bathroom taps (in older houses), and sometimes the central heating system make-up. Most tanks are round or rectangular polyethylene, holding 100-230 litres. Galvanised steel tanks predate 1970 and should be replaced if still in place: they corrode and contaminate the water supply. An annual inspection catches the two main failure modes: contamination and inadequate frost protection.

The lid: a close-fitting lid is required under the Water Regulations. A gap or missing lid allows dust, insects, and birds to enter the tank. A purpose-made insulated polythene lid is the minimum; a fitted board covered with rigid insulation is better.

The water surface: clear, clean, slightly blue-grey. Algae (green or brown bloom) indicates light reaching the water: the lid or insulation has a gap. Animal matter (drowned insect or mouse) is a contamination event and the tank needs emptying, disinfecting, and refilling.
The ball valve: should float at the correct water level (typically 50-75 mm below the top of the tank overflow pipe). A stuck ball valve causes overflow or underfill.
The tank body: check for cracks, bulging sides, or rust staining. Rust staining in a polyethylene tank means a metal fitting is corroding inside.
Pipe lagging: all cold pipes in the loft should be insulated with at least 25 mm foam lagging. Check the lagging at bends and fittings where it often splits or pulls away.

The tank itself needs insulation on all sides and the top, but NOT the base: heat rising from the room below through the loft hatch and ceiling provides a small but useful frost buffer. Wrapping the bottom seals off that heat source. Use a purpose-made tank jacket (rigid polyurethane pre-cut panels) or a 100 mm mineral wool blanket held in place with strapping.
Galvanised steel tanks: replace with polyethylene. Steel tanks corrode and the zinc lining is a contamination risk.
Cracked or brittle polyethylene: UV-degraded tanks (typically over 30 years old in an uninsulated loft) become fragile. Replace before they fail.
If converting to a combi boiler or a mains-pressure unvented cylinder: the loft tank is no longer needed. Remove it, cap the pipework, and close the inlet.
